one_line_takeaway: By encoding multi-bit messages into which vocabulary shards are boosted (via the message's majority bit) rather than shrinking the green list, MajorMark/MajorMark+ break the prior γ-vs-bit-accuracy trade-off, achieving both lower perplexity (e.g., 6.29 vs RSBH's 6.85 at b=64) and higher bit accuracy (e.g., 94.82% vs MPAC's 90.52%) than existing perturbation-based LLM watermarks, at practical (non-exponential) decoding cost.
key_finding: At b=64, MajorMark and MajorMark+ achieve average PPL of 6.57 and 6.29 respectively versus RSBH's 6.85 (improvements of +0.28 and +0.56, i.e., lower/better perplexity), and average BA of 92.03% and 94.82% versus MPAC's 90.52% (+1.50% and +4.30%), against a non-watermarked-text PPL floor of 3.81. Under low token budgets (T=400, b=64, δ=4), MajorMark+ reaches 95.78% BA versus MPAC's 89.22% and RSBH's 91.48% (+6.56% and +4.30%). Runtime at b=32: encoding overhead is negligible (~36s across all methods, dominated by generation itself); decoding takes 2.18s for MajorMark and 12.70s for MajorMark+, versus 0.13s for MPAC, 25.56s for RSBH, and an estimated ~8.89×10^8s for DepthW (exponential enumeration) — MajorMark+ is markedly more accurate than MPAC/RSBH at comparable or lower decoding cost than RSBH. Ablations show KMeans, Agglomerative Clustering, and GMM give comparable BA for MajorMark (all within ~4 points at δ=2, converging at δ=4), and r=2 is identified as the best BA/PPL tradeoff for MajorMark+ among r∈{1,2,4,8}. Under the Paraphrase attack, all methods degrade, and the paper reports MPAC (which uses a smaller γ) achieves higher BA than MajorMark+ at larger δ, with MajorMark slightly outperforming MajorMark+ under this attack due to its simpler decoding — an explicit case where the proposed methods do not universally dominate.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'majority bit-aware encoding,' a new multi-bit LLM watermark encoding paradigm that decouples watermark signal strength from green-list size. Instead of using a small green list (low γ = |G|/|V|) to get a detectable signal, the vocabulary is partitioned into b disjoint shards (one per message bit); the green list is the union of shards whose corresponding bit equals the message's majority bit λ. Because any b-bit message has a majority bit occurring in at least ceil(b/2) positions, this construction guarantees γ ≥ 0.5 by construction (proved in Theorem 1), while the specific set of shards chosen still encodes the message, shifting decoding from 'count green tokens' to 'infer which shards were boosted.' Two concrete instantiations are proposed: (1) MajorMark, which applies this directly per-token with a hash Hash(k, x_{t-1}, x_{t-2}, λ) and decodes via KMeans (K=2) clustering of shard-level token occurrence counts, recovering λ by testing both candidate values and picking the one giving the more skewed occurrence distribution; (2) MajorMark+, which splits the message into r blocks, applies majority-bit-aware encoding independently per block (each token contributes to one block, assigned via (x_{t-2}+x_{t-1}) mod r), incorporates the bit-occurrence count h_{λi} into the hash, and uses a deterministic (non-clustering) decoder that enumerates all (λi, h_{λi}) combinations per block to find the configuration producing the sharpest skew. Theorem 2 shows MajorMark+'s expected γ is even larger than MajorMark's (0.5 + 1/sqrt(2π(b/r)) vs 0.5 + 1/sqrt(2πb)), and its decoding cost is (b/r − 1)×|x|, avoiding the exponential 2^b enumeration required by prior brute-force multi-bit schemes (CTWL, CycleShift, DepthW).
threat_model: The setting (stated explicitly in the paper) is a cloud-based LLM-as-a-Service provider that embeds a b-bit message (e.g., user ID, model version, timestamp) into every generated output via a secret key k, in order to trace misuse of LLM-generated content (fake news, phishing, fraudulent reviews) back to a specific user/session. The adversary is a user who may edit the watermarked text before it is used maliciously; the paper evaluates robustness against two post-generation editing attacks: Copy-Paste (replacing 10% of the watermarked text with non-watermarked text) and Paraphrase (using the DIPPER paraphraser). It does not model an adversary who tries to forge/spoof another user's watermark (spoofing is only mentioned as deferred to an appendix) or one who has query access to probe the green-list structure (watermark-stealing style attacks are cited as related work but not evaluated as an attack in the main body).
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) the theoretical γ advantage of MajorMark shrinks as b grows large, converging toward 0.5, motivating the block-wise MajorMark+ variant; (2) two degenerate all-0 or all-1 messages must be explicitly excluded from the message space to avoid γ=1.0 (watermark becomes ineffective), though the fraction of excluded codes is stated as negligible (e.g., ~0.006% for b=32, r=2); (3) under the Paraphrase attack MajorMark+'s enumeration-based deterministic decoder is more sensitive to noisy token-occurrence statistics than MajorMark's clustering decoder, causing a measurable BA drop relative to MajorMark and, at high δ, relative to MPAC; (4) the paper defers evaluation against spoofing, sentence shuffling, and character-level perturbation attacks, and comparison against distortion-free watermarking methods, to the appendix rather than the main results (not verified in the extracted first-22-page text); (5) decoding still discards the first two generated tokens due to needing prompt-token context, a minor fixed overhead not separately quantified.

quantitative_results: b=64: MajorMark avg BA 92.03%, avg PPL 6.57; MajorMark+ avg BA 94.82%, avg PPL 6.29; RSBH avg PPL 6.85; MPAC avg BA 90.52%; non-watermarked PPL floor = 3.81
b=8: MajorMark avg PPL 6.06, MajorMark+ avg PPL 5.72 (best among all compared methods)
Token-budget test (b=64, δ=4, T=400): MPAC BA 89.22%, RSBH BA 91.48%, MajorMark+ BA 95.78%
Runtime at b=32: encoding ~36s for all methods; decoding — MPAC 0.13s, MajorMark 2.18s, RSBH 25.56s, MajorMark+ 12.70s, DepthW ≈8.89×10^8s (estimated)
Ablation (b=32): clustering choice for MajorMark — KMeans avg BA 97.92%/PPL 5.38, AC avg BA 96.95%, GMM avg BA 96.33% (all PPL 5.38, held fixed by δ)
Ablation (b=32): MajorMark+ block count r — r=1 avg BA 97.97%/PPL 5.65, r=2 avg BA 98.91%/PPL 5.27 (best balance), r=4 avg BA 94.22%/PPL 5.02, r=8 avg BA 95.24%/PPL 4.83
Theoretical green-list ratio: MajorMark E_m[γ] ≈ 0.5 + 1/sqrt(2πb); MajorMark+ E_m[γ] ≈ 0.5 + 1/sqrt(2π(b/r)); both guarantee γ ≥ 0.5 exactly (Theorems 1 and 2)
baselines_compared: CTWL (compared only in Table 1's complexity summary, not run experimentally in main text)
